task: Add ReferenceKind classifiers for 13 Tier-2 languages so tldr references emits call/definition/read/write/import/type instead of kind=other

assertions.text: For Java fixtures containing a method definition and two call sites, classify_reference_kind returns ReferenceKind::Definition for the definition identifier and ReferenceKind::Call for the two call sites. No ReferenceKind::Other for these core cases.
Same invariant for C: function_definition name = Definition, call_expression callee = Call.
Same invariant for C++: function_definition name = Definition, call_expression callee = Call. Qualified calls (ns::func()) also classify correctly.
Same for C#: method_declaration name = Definition, invocation_expression callee = Call.
Same for Kotlin: function_declaration name = Definition, call_expression callee = Call.
Same for Scala: function_definition/function_declaration name = Definition, call_expression callee = Call.
Same for Swift: function_declaration/init_declaration name = Definition, call_expression callee = Call.
Same for PHP: function_definition name = Definition, function_call_expression callee = Call.
Same for Ruby: method/singleton_method name = Definition, call callee = Call.
Same for Lua: function_declaration/function_definition_statement/local_function name = Definition, function_call callee = Call.
Same for Luau: function_declaration name = Definition, function_call callee = Call. (Luau grammar largely mirrors Lua.)

For Elixir's call-macro model: `def` / `defp` module-calls are Definition; direct function calls (bare atoms or aliased calls) are Call. Use the existing Elixir callgraph handler as reference for grammar kinds.
For OCaml: value_definition (let binding) name = Definition; application_expression callee = Call. Shadowing rebinding also emits Definition (not Write).
